paper_title: $τ$-Rec: A Verifiable Benchmark for Agentic Recommender Systems

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: As recommender systems transition toward agentic, multi-turn conversational interfaces, evaluation paradigms have struggled to keep pace. Current benchmarks often rely on "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluations, which introduce subjectivity, high costs and inconsistency. We present $τ$-Rec, a benchmark for agentic recommender systems that replaces subjective evaluation with verifiable rewards and a reveal-tagged elicitation (RTE) mechanism that controls how task constraints surface during dialogue. By testing agents against structured catalog predicates and employing a pass^k reliability metric, $τ$-Rec provides a systematic test for consistent reasoning. Our evaluation of nine configurations across five model families -- GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Qwen3-32B and GPT-5 mini -- reveals a steep reliability cliff, where even the best model achieves only ~57% at pass^1 and ~38% at pass^4, highlighting a critical gap in current conversational agent deployment. All code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/nbharaths/tau-rec.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 8 ] . [ 1 6 5 1 0 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r 𝜏-Rec: Verifiable Benchmark for Agentic Recommender Systems Bharath Sivaram Narasimhan Independent Researcher Mountain View, CA, USA nbharaths@gmail.com Karthik Narasimhan Princeton University Princeton, NJ, USA karthikn@princeton.edu Abstract As recommender systems transition toward agentic, multi-turn conversational interfaces, evaluation paradigms have struggled to keep pace. Current benchmarks often rely on LLM-as-a-judge evaluations, which introduce subjectivity, high costs and inconsistency. We present 𝝉-Rec, benchmark for agentic recommender systems that replaces subjective evaluation with verifiable rewards and reveal-tagged elicitation (RTE) mechanism that controls how task constraints surface during dialogue. By testing agents against structured catalog predicates and employing pass^k reliability metric, 𝜏-Rec provides systematic test for consistent reasoning. Our evaluation of nine configurations across five model families GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Qwen3-32B and GPT-5 mini reveals steep reliability cliff, where even the best model achieves only 57% at pass^1 and 35% at pass^4, highlighting critical gap in current conversational agent deployment. All code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/nbharaths/tau-rec.

sections.title:
1 Introduction
Modern recommender systems are evolving from static, single-
turn ranking pipelines into agentic systems where Large Language
Models (LLMs) drive multi-turn dialogues, invoke external tools to
gather context, and elicit user preferences progressively through
conversation. Recent systems such as InteRecAgent [8], RecMind
[20], MACRec [21], and AgentRecBench [16] illustrate this tra-
jectory in which agents now plan, query catalogs, reason over
multi-criteria constraints, and adapt within a session.
sections.content: However, evaluation of agentic recommender systems has not kept pace. Existing benchmarks fall into two camps, both inadequate for agentic settings. First, the static-dialogue camp, including works such as INSPIRED [7], DuRecDial [11], OpenDialKG [13], and CRSLab [26], measures recommendation quality with surface-level metrics like BLEU and Recall@k against fixed annotated dialogues, making them vulnerable to memorization [14, 23]. Second, the LLM-as-judge camp, including MT-Bench [24], UserSimCRS v2 [4] and CRS Arena [5], relies on subjective scoring that is expensive, non-deterministic, and inconsistent across runs. Bernard & Balog [3] further documented that existing metrics for conversational recommender systems (CRS) correlate only weakly with real user satisfaction. We propose 𝜏-Rec, verifiable benchmark for agentic conversational recommendation. 𝜏-Rec frames recommendation as constrained optimization problem in multi-turn dialogue and treats the interaction as Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) over tool-agent-user (TAU) loop. The recommender agent has access to catalog and metadata tools, while user simulator holds private preferences that the agent must elicit through conversation. The agent is tested simultaneously on two competencies: (i) preference elicitation asking the right questions to surface unstated user requirements, and (ii) constrained reasoning invoking the right tools and combining their results to satisfy the elicited constraints. Building on 𝜏-bench [22], we adopt the pass^k metric, which measures the probability of solving task correctly across all 𝑘 independent trials.1 pass^k surfaces reliability dimension that capability-only metrics like Recall@N and Hit Rate@N cannot detect, and brings reliability as new evaluation paradigm to agentic recommendation. We populate the data catalog with movies from TMDB that are post training cutoff for major LLMs. While our presentation focuses on movies, the framework is domain-agnostic and extends naturally to other domains like music, books, podcasts, or e-commerce. 𝜏-Rec also introduces policy-compliance objective absent from existing CRS benchmarks. Inspired by 𝜏-benchs domain-specific policy documents [22] and by MATCHAs [9] safety-policy work in game recommendation, our policies test not just what the agent recommends but how it recommends it, e.g., whether age-restricted content is appropriately gated, whether sponsored items are disclosed, whether already-consumed items are filtered, and whether the agent abstains on impossible requests rather than fabricating recommendation. This dimension makes responsible-AI behavior first-class evaluation target. We evaluate six contemporary models GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash, GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek V4 Flash and Qwen3-32B as agents, with GPT-5 mini as the user simulator. The results expose striking reliability cliff: even the strongest agent achieves only 57% at pass^1 and drops to 35% at pass^4, and the capability-latency Pareto frontier (Figure 1) shows GPT5.4 (no thinking) anchors the speed end while DeepSeek V4 Flash variants top the capability end.

sections.title:
2 Related Work
CRS benchmarks. Early CRS evaluation centered on static dia-
logue corpora — INSPIRED [7], DuRecDial [11], and OpenDialKG
[13] — consolidated under CRSLab [26]. These resources rely on
BLEU and Recall@k against fixed reference dialogues, measuring
surface similarity rather than task completion, and are exposed
to contamination [14, 23]. The closest agentic precedent, Agen-
tRecBench [16], evaluates LLM-agent recommendation over Ama-
zon, Goodreads, and Yelp, but its Hit Rate@N over 1-positive/19-
negative pools is a single-turn ranking metric, not multi-turn di-
alogue with a simulated user — and it has no pass^k, no policies,
and no fresh-catalog mechanism. CRS Arena [5] takes the com-
plementary route of crowdworker Elo battles and finds even the
sections.content: 1Although our default scoring requires every dimension to be satisfied for success, we also analyze partial-credit failure modes to attribute exactly which constraint or policy dimensions an agent fails on. Table 1: Comparison of CRS evaluation resources. = fully supported, = partial. VR=verifiable rewards, MT=multi-turn dialogue, TU=tool use, PC=policy checks, FC=fresh catalog, HI=hidden-intent simulation. Resource VR MT TU PC FC pass^k HI CRSLab [26] iEvaLM [19] UserSimCRS v2 [4] AgentRecBench [16] InteRecAgent [8] MATCHA [9] 𝜏-Rec (ours) best CRS satisfies users only 52% of the time. LiveCodeBench [10] and SciNUP [1] establish post-cutoff content as contamination defense in code and scholarly domains but no recommendation analog exists. Agentic and tool-use evaluation. 𝜏-bench[2, 22] is the closest methodological predecessor to 𝜏-Rec, evaluating tool-agent-user interaction with deterministic database-state verification, domainspecific policy documents, LLM-driven user simulators, and pass^k. Recommendation introduces fundamentally different challenges: preference elicitation under uncertainty, catalog exploration over large item space, multi-criteria optimization, and incremental intent revelation, requiring different tool APIs, different policy types, and different success criterion. ToolBench [15], AppWorld [18], and IFEval [25] also explore verifiable tool-use or instruction-following criteria but outside the recommendation setting. On the systems side, InteRecAgent [8], RecMind [20], and MACRec [21] build toolaugmented CRS architectures but evaluate themselves on Recall@K and NDCG@K over existing datasets without standardized tasks, policies, or pass^k. MATCHA [9] is the closest existing work to 𝜏Recs policy dimension it incorporates explicit safety/risk-control policies and reports 97.9% adversarial defense rate but it is deployed system on proprietary dataset, not reusable benchmark. RecBot [17] reports "pass rate" over 3-month A/B test, conceptually related to but less rigorous than pass^k. User simulation. iEvaLM [19] established LLM-driven user simulation for CRS but uses memorization-prone datasets, scores with Recall@k, and requires no tool use or policies. UserSimCRS v2 [4] supports agenda and LLM-based simulators with structured information needs, but assumes text-generating agents and treats policies as out of scope. ConvApparel [12] and RecUserSim [6] advance simulator validation without coupling to verifiable downstream tasks. None combine LLM role-play with reveal-tagged constraints the mechanism by which 𝜏-Rec forces agents to elicit information through dialogue rather than pattern-match against disclosed preference list. Table 1 summarizes how 𝜏-Rec combines features that prior resources provide only individually. 3 𝜏-Rec Benchmark Design 𝜏-Rec models the recommendation interaction as Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) over toolagentuser Narasimhan and Narasimhan (TAU) triad. At each turn, the recommender agent observes (i) the dialogue history with simulated user, (ii) the results of any tool calls it has issued so far, and (iii) the catalog metadata exposed through tool responses. The users full constraint setincluding hidden constraintsis unobservable and must be inferred through dialogue. The agents action space is the union of (a) natural-language utterances directed at the user, (b) catalog tool invocations (search, filter, metadata lookup, availability check), and (c) terminal recommend tool that submits single candidate. The episode terminates when the agent issues recommendation or turn budget is exhausted. The environment rests on four technical pillars, each motivated by specific failure mode of prior CRS evaluation. Verifiable rewards. Success is checked against typed catalog predicates such as runtime <= 120 or content_rating {PG-13, G}, all non-subjective judgments. The primary reward is the product constraint_score policy_score, both in [0, 1]. Under the strict scoring used in our experiments, recommendation succeeds only when every required constraint dimension is satisfied and every active policy is respected. We additionally report partial-credit decompositions to attribute failures to specific dimensions. Because scoring evaluates typed predicates directly against the catalog, it requires zero LLM calls and is fully deterministic and reproducible. Reveal-tagged elicitation (RTE). Each constraint in task is tagged as volunteer (the user states it proactively in the opening turn), on_ask (the user states it only when explicitly asked about that attribute), or hidden (the user never states it explicitly but rejects recommendations that violate it). This tagging forces the agent to elicit information through dialogue rather than receive complete preference dump in the first turn, closing the loophole that has made many existing CRS benchmarks easy to game by single-shot pattern matching. pass^k reliability metric. Following 𝜏-bench [22], we report pass^k, the probability that the agent solves task on all 𝑘 independent trials, rather than mean success across trials. pass^k = 1 𝑇 𝑡 𝑇 (cid:1) (cid:1) (cid:0)𝑐𝑡 𝑘 (cid:0)𝑛𝑡 𝑘 pass^1 measures capability; pass^k for 𝑘 > 1 measures consistency. As Bernard & Balog [3] argued, capability-only metrics conceal reliability cliffs that surface when an agent must succeed repeatedly. pass^k is, to our knowledge, novel to recommendation evaluation. Policy enforcement as first-class objective. Each task carries list of active policy flags drawn from domain-specific policy document. Programmatic checks audit the trajectory for compliance with seven policies: recommend_tool (the agent must issue its recommendation through the dedicated tool, not in free text), watch_history (do not recommend already-consumed items), availability (only recommend titles available on the users stated streaming services), age_restricted (gate adult content for younger personas), sponsored (disclose sponsored placement), transparency (on impossible tasks, explicitly abstain rather than force nonexistent recommendation), and single_recommendation (return single concrete recommendation, not list). Policy compliance is what differentiates 𝜏-Rec from AgentRecBench [16] since we test not only what the agent recommends but how it conducts the recommendation interaction. 𝜏-Rec: Verifiable Benchmark for Agentic Recommender Systems

sections.title:
3.1 Movie Catalog Construction
The benchmark catalog is sourced from The Movie Database (TMDB)
via its public REST API. Our construction pipeline has three stages:
Stage 1: Discovery. We query TMDB’s /discover/movie end-
point with date filters drawing from 2025–2026 releases to ensure
post-training cutoffs for all major LLMs, sorting by popularity and
vote count to surface titles with sufficient metadata coverage.
sections.content: Stage 2: Enrichment. For each candidate, we issue follow-up requests to retrieve full metadata: genre tags, runtime in minutes, MPAA-style content rating (G / PG / PG-13 / / NR), cast and director, vote average and vote count (used as quality proxy), release date, and per-region streaming-provider information from /movie/{id}/watch/providers (e.g., Netflix, Prime Video, Hulu, Disney+, Apple TV+). Stage 3: Normalization and validation. Records are normalized to typed schema, lightly deduplicated, and filtered to ensure that all task-relevant attributes (runtime, genres, content_rating, streaming_services, rating) are populated. Items missing any required field are dropped to guarantee that constraint predicates can be evaluated cleanly. The current catalog contains 153 movies, and can easily be extended. Each catalog entry exposes the typed fields above, which together form the predicate basis for constraint verification. The pipeline is parameterized and idempotent, allowing regular refresh to produce new catalog snapshot pinned to date, and all task constraints can be automatically re-validated against the new catalog to confirm task solvability remains intact.

sections.title:
3.2 Task Construction
We authored tasks using a structured protocol. Each task specifies:
sections.content: (1) natural-language persona for the simulated user (e.g., tired parent looking for short comedy after their kids are asleep). (2) set of typed constraints over the catalog schema (genres, runtime bounds, content-rating allow-list, minimum vote average, required streaming services), each with an RTE tag volunteer / on_ask / hidden. (3) Optional soft preferences used by the simulator to style its responses (e.g., prefer recent films, but its not hard rule), but not scored against the agents recommendation. (4) list of active policy flags that the agents behavior must respect over the course of the trajectory. We stratify the tasks along two axes. Complexity is set by constraint count, either simple (12 constraints), medium (34), or complex (5+). Reveal difficulty is set at the task level: volunteer tasks expose all constraints proactively, mixed tasks include at least one on_ask constraint, and hidden tasks include at least one constraint the simulator never states explicitly. The current release comprises 60 tasks across 3 3 complexity (20 simple / 24 medium / 16 complex) reveal-difficulty (13 volunteer / 32 mixed / 15 hidden) grid, including 5 no-valid-recommendation tasks (where no catalog item satisfies the full constraint set) to test whether agents correctly abstain rather than fabricate recommendation.

sections.title:
4 Benchmark Validation
We evaluated nine configurations (six base models, with GPT-5.4
run in two thinking modes and DeepSeek V4 Flash run in three
thinking modes) on all 60 tasks across 4 trials each, with GPT-5 mini
as the simulator2 (Table 2). We use pass^k to measure reliability as
the probability an agent succeeds on all 𝑘 trials of a task. All runs
use LiteLLM with temp=0 for agent models (where supported) and
temp=1.0 for the simulator.
sections.content: Main results. GPT-5.4, Sonnet 4.6, and DeepSeek V4 Flash variants form top tier (pass^1: 0.5370.571) with overlapping confidence intervals. All models have lower pass^2 and pass^4 scores demonstrating lack of consistency in their agentic performance. Policy Compliance. Policy compliance varies sharply across the model lineup (Table 2, Policy column). The strongest tier (GPT-5.4 with thinking, GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek V4 Flash) has >0.92 compliance, while Qwen3-32B falls to 0.756 with 0.21 availability violation rate, where the model recommends titles without verifying streaming-service eligibility. Efficiency. Tool use varies considerably across models, from DeepSeek V4 Flashs 28 median calls per trial to Qwen3-32Bs 5 and Gemini 2.5 Flashs 8. The high-volume models (DS V4 Flash, GPT5.4 with thinking, Sonnet 4.6) take more turns to recommendation but achieve higher constraint scores. Failure Analysis. From table 2, we identify three failure modes. Abstainers (Qwen3-32B, GPT-5 mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash) fail by not committing, having No-rec rates of 0.43, 0.45, 0.67 with single-digit median tool calls; they exit without issuing recommend(). Wrongcommitters commit confidently to constraint-violating recommendations: Qwen3-32Bs 0.21 availability-violation rate coupled with high commitment is the clearest case. On the other hand, balanced models (DeepSeek V4 Flash, GPT-5.4 with thinking, Sonnet 4.6) invest 1528 median tool calls and commit, yielding the highest constraint scores (0.570.58). pass^k amplifies these distinctions and under repeated trials, neither hedging nor bluffing converges, and the cliff steepens. Difficulty gradient. The reveal-tag mechanism produces steep difficulty gradient that holds across all models  (Table 3)  . For DeepSeek V4 Flash, pass^1 drops from 0.846 on volunteer tasks (constraints stated up front) to 0.586 on mixed tasks (one or more on_ask constraints) to 0.200 on hidden tasks (one or more hidden constraints) 4 gap purely from how information is revealed. The same gradient appears for GPT-5 mini (0.712 / 0.414 / 0.167) and Qwen3-32B (0.481 / 0.281 / 0.067). Hidden constraints expose the elicitation-vs-retrieval distinction: agents that excel at constraintsatisfaction over known preference set struggle when the preference must first be inferred from rejection signals. Capability-latency frontier. We also analyze the capability vs latency trade-offs for different models, since per-turn latency is an important consideration for any agentic RS (Figure 1). GPT-5.4 (no thinking) anchors the fast end of the frontier (pass^1 = 0.47 at 7s/step), while Sonnet 4.6 (15s) and the DeepSeek V4 Flash variants (1524s) trace the rest of the frontier, topping out at pass^1 = 0.57 for DS V4 Flash (max thinking). Off-frontier models 2We ran subset of trials with various simulators and found GPT-5 mini to provide good performance at reasonable cost. Table 2: Per-model reliability (pass^k = probability of succeeding on all 𝑘 trials), mean constraint/policy sub-scores, top policy violation rates (avail.=availability, w.hist.=watch history, transp.=transparency, No-rec=no recommendation issued), and efficiency (Turns = mean turns to recommendation, Tools = median tool calls per task). 95% bootstrap CIs on pass^4: 0.100.13. Reveal GPT-5.4 GPT-5.4+T Sonnet 4.6 Gem-2.5F DS-V4F DS-V4F+T(hi) DS-V4F+T(max) Qwen3-32B GPT-5 mini Volunteer (13) Mixed (32) Hidden (15) 0.750 0.477 0.217 0.891 0.516 0. 0.923 0.523 0.233 0.500 0.289 0.050 0.846 0.586 0.200 0.827 0.609 0.222 0.865 0.586 0.283 0.481 0.281 0. 0.712 0.414 0.167 Single domain. While the framework is domain-agnostic, our released benchmark covers movies only. Extending to other domains is straightforward given the typed-predicate scoring; cross-domain task suites and larger-𝑘 runs are natural next steps. Statistical power. We report 4 trials per task; 95% bootstrap confidence intervals on pass^4 are non-trivial (0.100.13). Distinguishing models with overlapping CIs would require either more trials per task (cost-bound) or more tasks per cell (annotation-bound). Thinking modes follow shallow, sub-linear trajectories, e.g. DS V4 Flash gains only +0.025 pass^1 from non-thinking to max-effort thinking, suggesting current models are capability constrained for this benchmark.

sections.title:
5 Limitations and Future Work
Catalog scale. The catalog is intentionally small (153 titles) to
isolate reasoning ability from retrieval scale — agents access it via
API tools, so size is opaque to the model — but limits failure-mode
diversity; scaling while preserving post-cutoff freshness is future
work.
